author: orion3336
content:
re rust native: Not really only exclusively possible with rust native but much cleaner and strong ownership guarantees! With bindings, even zero-copy FFI doesn't save you from maintaining two memory models, you still risk duplicates when the orchestrator needs to inspect, route, or transform witness data. Rust-native keeps you in a single memory model throughout, so no boundary tax, no duplicate risk, and ownership guarantees intact.

on zk proof needs:
Sure thing! From our experience the main needs that drove the design were:

Barrier guarantees: splitting then reconstructing recursive and aggregate proofs in batch with low data overhead.
Multi-queue pipelines: we needed to commit to batch metadata before proofs were ready, finalizing boundaries optimistically and only reverting on failure. This gave us 4x+ improvement over a linear pipeline that blocks waiting for proofs.
Custom collector for ZK-specific synchronisation: all the scheduling semantics ZK proofs need (barrier completion, partition-affine routing, pluggable strategies) are streamlined into the collector abstraction with minimal config changes.

You could probably build this on Ray, but building them natively made more sense than layering them on top of general primitives.

re benchmarking:
We covered latency analysis, fault injection, and memory evaluation in the paper. Our thesis was simple a good orchestrator has low overhead and recovers fast. From our experience network topology and regions have more impact on orchestration overhead than proving hardware, since the orchestrator itself is agnostic to what the prover is doing. Happy to start throwing ideas in a doc and extend it together. Curious what your take is though.. do you see proving hardware having a meaningful impact on orchestration overhead?
